title: Adding a Hidden Layer
concept: Resolving the XOR cliffhanger: instead of one perceptron drawing one straight line, a hidden layer of perceptrons combines several lines into a bent boundary that finally separates XOR.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What idea was hiding in plain sight as the fix for the XOR problem?

options.text: Connecting the outputs of multiple perceptrons together into layers
Making the perceptron's line thicker so it covers more area
Switching from true/false labels to numbers

exercises.explain: Exactly — the fix was combining perceptrons into layers, not fixing one perceptron alone.

cards.title: Let's actually build it
cards.body: Last time you found the clue: don't rely on one perceptron, stack them. Today you stop imagining it and actually build the stack that solves XOR.

cards.title: What is a hidden layer?
cards.body: A hidden layer is a group of perceptrons sitting between the input and the final output perceptron. They're called "hidden" because their answers aren't the network's final answer — they're passed along as ingredients for the next perceptron to use.

cards.title: An assembly line, not a lone worker
cards.body: One perceptron working alone is like one worker doing an entire job start to finish. A hidden layer is an assembly line: each perceptron handles one small piece, then hands its result to the next station — the output perceptron — for final assembly.

cards.title: OR, NAND, then AND
cards.body: Give one hidden perceptron the job of learning OR, and a second hidden perceptron the job of learning NAND. Feed both of their answers into a final output perceptron that learns AND. Check every input pair against the XOR table — it matches, perfectly.

cards.title: "Hidden" doesn't mean magic
cards.body: "Hidden layer" sounds mysterious, like it's doing something secret. It isn't — it's just a normal layer of perceptrons that sits in the middle of the network, unseen only in the sense that it's not the input and not the final output.

cards.title: One line vs. a bent boundary
cards.body: What adding a hidden layer buys you:

left.text:
One straight line
Stuck failing at XOR

right.text:
Lines combined together
Boundary bends to fit XOR

options.text: A group of perceptrons between the input and the final output perceptron
The very first perceptron that sees the raw input
A layer that is deleted once the network works
A special output that predicts the future

exercises.explain: A hidden layer sits between input and output, passing its results along rather than being the final answer.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A hidden layer's output is always the network's final answer.
exercises.explain: False — the hidden layer's job is to feed its results forward into the next perceptron, not to be the final answer itself.

exercises.sentence: In the XOR fix, one hidden perceptron learns OR, another learns NAND, and the output perceptron combines them using ___.
exercises.explain: Combining the OR and NAND answers with AND reproduces the XOR pattern exactly.

exercises.prompt: Which network design could realistically bend its boundary enough to solve XOR?

options.text: One hidden perceptron layer feeding into an output perceptron
The same single perceptron trained twice

exercises.explain: Stacking perceptrons into a hidden layer is what lets the boundary bend — training one perceptron twice still only ever draws one line.

exercises.prompt: A colleague says: "I'll just retrain my single perceptron for way longer, it'll eventually learn XOR." Using what you now know about hidden layers, what's wrong with their plan?

options.text: No amount of training helps — a single perceptron can only ever draw one straight line, no matter how long it trains; XOR needs a hidden layer to bend the boundary
Nothing is wrong, longer training always fixes any problem
They're right, but only if they also delete half of the XOR truth table
The problem is the data, not the perceptron, so they should collect more examples

exercises.explain: Right! The limitation is structural, not a training-time problem. A lone perceptron is capped at one straight line forever — only stacking perceptrons into a hidden layer can bend that boundary to fit XOR.
